metadata.title: Bioanalytical Method Validation: Guidance for Industry

rules.rule_text: Pivotal studies submitted in an NDA, BLA, or ANDA that require regulatory decision making for approval, safety or labeling, such as BE or pharmacokinetic studies, must include bioanalytical methods that are fully validated.
rules.source_section: Section II, Background / Fit-for-Purpose Concept
rules.verbatim_quote: Pivotal studies submitted in an NDA, BLA, or ANDA that require regulatory decision making for approval, safety or labeling, such as BE or pharmacokinetic studies, should include bioanalytical methods that are fully validated.
rules.exceptions: Exploratory methods used for candidate selection and not supporting regulatory decisions may not require stringent validation.

rules.rule_text: The analytical laboratory conducting toxicology studies for regulatory submissions must adhere to 21 CFR 58, Good Laboratory Practices (GLPs). The bioanalytical method for human BA, BE, and pharmacokinetic studies must meet the criteria specified in 21 CFR 320 Bioequivalence and Bioavailability Requirements (i.e., 21 CFR 320.29).
rules.source_section: Section II, Background
rules.verbatim_quote: The analytical laboratory conducting toxicology studies for regulatory submissions should adhere to 21 CFR 58, Good Laboratory Practices (GLPs). The bioanalytical method for human BA, BE, and pharmacokinetic studies must meet the criteria specified in 21 CFR 320 Bioequivalence and Bioavailability Requirements (i.e., 21 CFR 320.29).

rules.rule_text: The sponsor must conduct a full validation of any new bioanalytical method for the analysis of a new drug entity, its metabolite(s), or biomarkers.
rules.source_section: Section III.A, Guiding Principles
rules.verbatim_quote: Conduct a full validation of any new bioanalytical method for the analysis of a new drug entity, its metabolite(s), or biomarkers.

rules.rule_text: The sponsor must conduct a full validation for any revisions to an existing validated method that adds a metabolite or an additional analyte.
rules.source_section: Section III.A, Guiding Principles
rules.verbatim_quote: Conduct a full validation for any revisions to an existing validated method that adds a metabolite or an additional analyte.

rules.rule_text: The sponsor must establish a detailed, written description (e.g., protocol, study plan, and/or standard operating procedure (SOP)) for the bioanalytical method before initiating validation. The description must identify procedures that control critical parameters in the method from the time of collection to the time of analysis.
rules.source_section: Section III.A, Guiding Principles
rules.verbatim_quote: Establish a detailed, written description (e.g., protocol, study plan, and/or standard operating procedure (SOP)) for the bioanalytical method before initiating validation. The description should identify procedures that control critical parameters in the method (e.g., environmental, matrix, procedural variables) from the time of collection of the samples to the time of analysis to minimize their effects on the measurement of the analyte in the matrix.

rules.rule_text: The sponsor must document and report (in the method validation report) all experiments used to make claims or draw conclusions about the validity of the method.
rules.source_section: Section III.A, Guiding Principles
rules.verbatim_quote: Document and report (in the method validation report) all experiments used to make claims or draw conclusions about the validity of the method.

rules.rule_text: The sponsor must use authenticated analytical reference standards with known identities and purities to prepare solutions of known concentrations. The reference standard should be identical to the analyte; when not possible, an established chemical form (e.g., free base, free acid, or salt) of known purity may be used.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.1.a, Reference Standards
rules.verbatim_quote: The sponsor should use authenticated analytical reference standards with known identities and purities to prepare solutions of known concentrations. The reference standard should be identical to the analyte; however, when this scenario is not possible, the sponsor can use an established chemical form (e.g., free base, free acid, or salt) of known purity.
rules.exceptions: When reference standard identical to analyte is not possible, established chemical form of known purity may be used.

rules.rule_text: The sponsor must provide certificates of analyses (CoA), including source, lot number, and expiration date for commercially available reference standards. For internally or externally generated reference standards without a CoA, evidence of identity and purity must be provided.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.1.a, Reference Standards
rules.verbatim_quote: The sponsor should provide the certificates of analyses (CoA), including the source, lot number, and expiration date (with the exception of United States Pharmacopeia (USP) standards) for commercially available reference standards. For internally or externally generated reference standards that do not have a CoA, the sponsor should provide evidence of the standard's identity and purity in addition to the source and the lot number.
rules.exceptions: USP standards are excepted from the expiration date requirement.

rules.rule_text: For calibration curve (CC) validation: non-zero calibrators must be within ±15% of nominal (theoretical) concentrations, except at LLOQ where the calibrator must be within ±20% of the nominal concentrations. A minimum of 75% and at least six non-zero calibrator levels must meet these criteria in each validation run.
rules.source_section: Section VII, Table 1 – Calibration Curve Acceptance Criteria (CCs)
rules.verbatim_quote: Non-zero calibrators should be ± 15% of nominal (theoretical) concentrations, except at LLOQ where the calibrator should be ± 20% of the nominal concentrations in each validation run. 75% and a minimum of six non-zero calibrator levels should meet the above criteria in each validation run.
rules.exceptions: Only data points that fail to meet acceptance criteria may be excluded; exclusion should not change the model used.

rules.rule_text: For calibration curve (LBA) validation: non-zero calibrators must be within ±20% of nominal concentrations, except at LLOQ and ULOQ where the calibrator must be within ±25% of nominal. A minimum of 75% and at least six non-zero calibrator levels must meet these criteria. Anchor points must not be included in the curve fit.
rules.source_section: Section VII, Table 1 – Calibration Curve Acceptance Criteria (LBAs)
rules.verbatim_quote: Non-zero calibrators should be ± 20% of nominal (theoretical) concentrations, except at LLOQ and ULOQ where the calibrator should be ± 25% of the nominal concentrations in each validation run. 75% and a minimum of six non-zero calibrator levels should meet the above criteria in each validation run. Anchor points should not be included in the curve fit.
rules.exceptions: Anchor points may be used to facilitate curve fitting but must not be used as acceptance criteria.

rules.rule_text: For in-study CC analysis, non-zero calibrators must be within ±15% (CC) or ±20% (LBA) of nominal concentrations, except at LLOQ where ±20% (CC) or ±25% (LBA) applies. A minimum of 75% and at least six non-zero calibrator levels must meet these criteria.
rules.source_section: Section VII, Table 1 – Calibration Curve In-Study Analysis Recommendations
rules.verbatim_quote: CC: Non-zero calibrators should be ± 15%, except at LLOQ where the calibrator should be ± 20% of nominal concentrations in each run. LBA: Non-zero calibrators should be ± 20%, except at LLOQ and ULOQ where the calibrator should be ± 25% of nominal concentrations in each run. CC and LBA: 75% and a minimum of six non-zero calibrator levels should meet the above criteria in each run.

rules.rule_text: For CC validation A&P runs, four QCs are required including LLOQ, low (3x LLOQ), mid (mid-range), and high (high-range) from at least five replicates in at least three runs. For LBA validation A&P runs, five QCs including LLOQ, L, M, H, and ULOQ from at least three replicates in at least six runs are required.
rules.source_section: Section VII, Table 1 – Quality Controls (QC) Elements
rules.verbatim_quote: For A & P Runs: Four QCs, including LLOQ, low (L: defined as three times the LLOQ), mid (M: defined as mid-range), and high (H: defined as high-range) from at least five replicates in at least three runs... For A& P Runs: Five QCs, including LLOQ, L, M, H, and ULOQ from at least three replicates in at least six runs.

rules.rule_text: For in-study QC acceptance criteria: CC assays require ≥67% of QCs to be within ±15% of nominal, and ≥50% of QCs per level to be within ±15%. LBA assays require ≥67% of QCs to be within ±20% of nominal, and ≥50% of QCs per level to be within ±20%.
rules.source_section: Section VII, Table 1 – Quality Controls Acceptance Criteria (In-Study)
rules.verbatim_quote: CC: ≥ 67% of QCs should be ± 15% of the nominal, and ≥ 50% of QCs per level should be ± 15% of their nominal. LBA: ≥ 67% of QCs should be ± 20% of the nominal, and ≥ 50% of QCs per level should be ± 20% of their nominal.

rules.rule_text: For CC selectivity validation: blank and zero calibrators must be free of interference at retention times of the analyte(s) and IS; spiked samples must be within ±20% of LLOQ; IS response in the blank must not exceed 5% of average IS responses of calibrators and QCs. Blank samples from at least six individual sources must be analyzed.
rules.source_section: Section VII, Table 1 – Selectivity Acceptance Criteria (CCs)
rules.verbatim_quote: Analyze blank samples of the appropriate biological matrix from at least six individual sources... Blank and zero calibrators should be free of interference at the retention times of the analyte(s) and the IS. Spiked samples should be ± 20% LLOQ. The IS response in the blank should not exceed 5% of the average IS responses of the calibrators and QCs.

rules.rule_text: For LBA selectivity validation: blank samples from ≥10 individual sources must be analyzed. For ≥80% of sources, unspiked matrix must be BQL, spiked samples must be within ±25% at LLOQ, and within ±20% at H QC.
rules.source_section: Section VII, Table 1 – Selectivity Acceptance Criteria (LBAs)
rules.verbatim_quote: Conduct an analysis of blank samples in the matrix from ≥ 10 individual sources... For ≥ 80% of sources, unspiked matrix should be BQL, and spiked samples should be ± 25% at LLOQ, and ± 20% at H QC.

rules.rule_text: Carryover must not exceed 20% of the LLOQ for chromatographic assays (CCs). This applies to both validation and in-study analysis.
rules.source_section: Section VII, Table 1 – Carryover Acceptance Criteria
rules.verbatim_quote: Carryover should not exceed 20% of LLOQ.
rules.exceptions: Not applicable to LBAs for validation; carryover should be monitored in-study for LBAs.

rules.rule_text: For CC sensitivity (LLOQ) validation: the analyte response at the LLOQ must be ≥5 times the analyte response of the zero calibrator; accuracy must be ±20% of nominal concentration (from ≥5 replicates in at least three runs); precision must be ±20% CV. For LBA: accuracy must be ±25% of nominal (from ≥3 replicates in at least six runs); precision must be ±25% CV; total error must be ≤40%.
rules.source_section: Section VII, Table 1 – Sensitivity Acceptance Criteria
rules.verbatim_quote: The analyte response at the LLOQ should be ≥ five times the analyte response of the zero calibrator. The accuracy should be ± 20% of nominal concentration (from ≥ five replicates in at least three runs). The precision should be ± 20% CV (from ≥ five replicates in at least three runs)... [LBA] The accuracy should be ± 25% of the nominal concentration (from ≥ three replicates in at least six runs). The precision should be ± 25% CV (from ≥ three replicates in at least six runs). The total error should be ≤ 40%.

rules.rule_text: For CC accuracy and precision (A&P) validation: within-run and between-run accuracy must be ±15% of nominal concentrations (except ±20% at LLOQ); precision must be ±15% CV (except ±20% CV at LLOQ). A&P must be established with at least three independent A&P runs, four QC levels (LLOQ, L, M, H), and ≥5 replicates per QC level.
rules.source_section: Section VII, Table 1 – Accuracy and Precision Acceptance Criteria (CCs)
rules.verbatim_quote: A & P should be established with at least three independent A & P runs, four QC levels per run (LLOQ, L, M, H QC), and ≥ five replicates per QC level... Accuracy: Within-run and between runs: ± 15% of nominal concentrations; except ± 20% at LLOQ. Precision: Within-run and between runs: ± 15% CV, except ± 20% CV at LLOQ.

rules.rule_text: For LBA accuracy and precision (A&P) validation: within-run and between-run accuracy must be ±20% of nominal concentrations (except ±25% at LLOQ and ULOQ); precision must be ±20% CV (except ±25% CV at LLOQ and ULOQ); total error must be ±30% (except ±40% at LLOQ and ULOQ). A&P must be established with at least six independent runs, five QC levels (LLOQ, L, M, H, ULOQ), and ≥3 replicates per QC level.
rules.source_section: Section VII, Table 1 – Accuracy and Precision Acceptance Criteria (LBAs)
rules.verbatim_quote: A & P should be established with at least six independent A & P runs, five QC levels per run (LLOQ, L, M, H, ULOQ QC), and ≥ three replicates per QC level... Accuracy: Within-run and between runs: ± 20% of nominal concentrations; except ± 25% at LLOQ, ULOQ. Precision: Within-run and between runs: ± 20% CV, ± 25% at LLOQ, ULOQ. Total Error: QCs should be ± 30%, except at LLOQ, ULOQ ± 40%.

rules.rule_text: For in-study accuracy and precision: CC accuracy (between runs) must be ±15% of nominal concentrations; LBA accuracy (between runs) must be ±20%. CC precision (between runs) must be ±15% CV; LBA precision must be ±20% CV.
rules.source_section: Section VII, Table 1 – Accuracy and Precision In-Study Recommendations
rules.verbatim_quote: Accuracy: Between runs: CC: ± 15% of nominal concentrations; LBA: ± 20% of nominal concentrations. Precision: Between runs: CC: ± 15% CV; LBA: ±20% CV.

rules.rule_text: For stability validation: CC stability accuracy (% nominal) at each level must be ±15%. LBA stability accuracy at each level must be ±20%. Stability must be assessed for auto-sampler, bench-top, extract/processed sample, freeze-thaw (minimum three cycles), and long-term conditions at L and H QC concentrations (at least three replicates).
rules.source_section: Section VII, Table 1 – Stability Acceptance Criteria
rules.verbatim_quote: For auto-sampler, bench-top, extract, freeze-thaw, stock solution and long-term stability, perform at least three replicates at L and H QC concentrations... The accuracy (% nominal) at each level should be ± 15% [CC]... The accuracy (% nominal) at each level should be ± 20% [LBA].
rules.exceptions: Long-term stability must cover the time between first sample collection and last sample analysis.

rules.rule_text: Freeze-thaw stability must be assessed for a minimum of three freeze-thaw cycles. QC samples must be thawed and analyzed according to the same procedures as the study samples, and must be frozen for at least 12 hours between cycles.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.7, Stability – Freeze-thaw stability
rules.verbatim_quote: Freeze-thaw stability: The sponsor should assess the stability of the sample after a minimum of three freeze-thaw cycles. QC samples should be thawed and analyzed according to the same procedures as the study samples. QC samples should be frozen for at least 12 hours between cycles.

rules.rule_text: Long-term stability of samples must be determined over a period equal to or exceeding the time between first sample collection and last sample analysis. Storage temperatures studied must be the same as those used to store study samples.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.7, Stability – Long-term stability
rules.verbatim_quote: Long-term stability: The sponsor should determine the long-term stability of the sample over a period of time equal to or exceeding the time between the date of first sample collection and the date of last sample analysis. The storage temperatures studied should be the same as those used to store study samples.

rules.rule_text: For dilution integrity validation in CCs: QCs for planned dilutions must include 5 replicates per dilution factor, with accuracy ±15% of nominal concentrations and precision ±15% CV. For LBAs: dilution linearity and lack of prozone effect must be demonstrated, with accuracy ±20% and precision ±20% CV using 5 replicates per dilution factor.
rules.source_section: Section VII, Table 1 – Dilution Acceptance Criteria
rules.verbatim_quote: QCs for planned dilutions, 5 replicates per dilution factor: Accuracy: ± 15% of nominal concentrations; Precision: ± 15% CV [CC]... 5 replicates per dilution factor: Accuracy: ± 20% of nominal concentrations; Precision: ± 20% CV [LBA].
rules.exceptions: LBAs must also demonstrate lack of prozone effect.

rules.rule_text: Incurred Sample Reanalysis (ISR) must be performed in all studies submitted in an NDA, BLA, or ANDA that provide pivotal data for the approval or labeling of the product. ISR sample selection must cover 10% reanalysis of the first 1000 samples and 5% reanalysis of remaining samples, selected around Cmax and in the elimination phase.
rules.source_section: Section IV, Incurred Sample Reanalysis; Table 1 – ISR Elements
rules.verbatim_quote: ISR should be conducted in all studies submitted in an NDA, BLA, or ANDA that provide pivotal data for the approval or labeling of the product, regardless of the matrix... Sample size: 10% reanalysis of the first 1000 samples, and 5% reanalysis of the remaining samples. Sample selection: Around Cmax and in the elimination phase.
rules.exceptions: For nonclinical safety studies, the performing laboratory should conduct ISR at least once for each method and species.

rules.rule_text: ISR acceptance criteria: for CC assays, 67% of reanalyzed samples must be within ±20% of the mean of original and repeat results. For LBA assays, 67% of reanalyzed samples must be within ±30% of the mean.
rules.source_section: Section VII, Table 1 – ISR Acceptance Criteria
rules.verbatim_quote: Acceptance Criteria: CC: 67% should be ± 20% of the mean; LBA: 67% should be ± 30% of the mean.

rules.rule_text: Repeat analysis is only acceptable for assignable causes (e.g., samples above the ULOQ, sample processing errors, equipment failure, poor chromatography). The SOP for repeat analysis must be established a priori, must include acceptance criteria, and final values must be reported. No re-analysis of individual calibrators and QCs is permitted.
rules.source_section: Section III.C, Validated Methods / Repeat Analysis; Table 1 – Repeat Analysis
rules.verbatim_quote: Repeat analysis is acceptable only for assignable causes (e.g., the samples are above the ULOQ, there are sample processing errors, there is an equipment failure, the chromatography is poor). The SOP should include the acceptance criteria for re-analysis, and the sponsor or applicant should report final values... No re-analysis of individual calibrators and QCs is permitted.
rules.exceptions: No confirmatory repeats are permitted for BE studies.

rules.rule_text: Total QCs in each analytical run must number at least five percent of total samples analyzed, or at least six in number (low-, mid-, and high-QCs in duplicate), whichever is greater. QCs must be interspersed with study samples during processing and analysis.
rules.source_section: Section III.C, Validated Methods: Expectations of In-Study Analysis and Reporting
rules.verbatim_quote: Total QCs should number at least five percent of the total samples analyzed, or be at least six in number (low-, mid-, and high-QCs, in duplicate), whichever is greater... The QCs should be interspersed with study samples during processing and analysis.

rules.rule_text: Study samples with concentrations listed below the LLOQ must be reported as below the LLOQ (BQL). Study samples with concentrations above the ULOQ must be diluted and re-analyzed, or the standard curve must be extended and revalidated.
rules.source_section: Section III.C, Validated Methods: Expectations of In-Study Analysis and Reporting
rules.verbatim_quote: Study samples with concentrations listed below the LLOQ should be reported as below the LLOQ (BQL). Study samples with concentrations above the ULOQ should be diluted and re-analyzed, or the standard curve should be extended and revalidated.

rules.rule_text: Assays of all study samples of an analyte in a biological matrix must be completed within the time period for which stability data are available. If sample handling conditions are changed or exceed validated stability data, stability must be re-established at the new conditions.
rules.source_section: Section III.C, Validated Methods: Expectations of In-Study Analysis and Reporting
rules.verbatim_quote: Assays of all study samples of an analyte in a biological matrix should be completed within the time period for which stability data are available. If sample handling conditions are changed or exceed validated stability data, then the stability of the sample should be established at the new conditions.

rules.rule_text: All study samples from a subject must be analyzed in a single run, especially for studies designed with repeated measures (e.g., crossover or sequential design required for BE studies). If other approaches are taken, the sponsor must justify the approach and take steps to minimize variability between periods.
rules.source_section: Section III.C, Validated Methods: Expectations of In-Study Analysis and Reporting
rules.verbatim_quote: All study samples from a subject should be analyzed in a single run, especially for studies designed with repeated measures from individual subjects (e.g., crossover or sequential design required for BE studies). If other approaches are taken, the sponsor or applicant should justify the approach and take steps to minimize the variability between periods.

rules.rule_text: An SOP or guideline for sample data reintegration for CCs must be established a priori. The SOP must define criteria for re-integration, document rationale clearly, maintain audit trails, and document and report original and re-integrated data.
rules.source_section: Section III.C, Validated Methods: Expectations of In-Study Analysis and Reporting
rules.verbatim_quote: An SOP or guideline for sample data reintegration for CCs should be established a priori. This SOP or guideline should define the criteria for re-integration and how the re-integration will be performed. The rationale for the re-integration should be clearly described and documented. Audit trails should be maintained. Original and re-integrated data should be documented and reported.

rules.rule_text: When changes are made to a validated method, the sponsor must conduct additional validation (i.e., partial or cross validation). Partial validation is required for method modifications including laboratory transfers, changes in analytical methodology, sample processing, sample volume, instruments/software platforms, matrix, anticoagulant, or LBA critical reagents.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.9.a, Partial Validation
rules.verbatim_quote: Partial validations evaluate modifications of already validated bioanalytical methods. Partial validation can range from as little as one intra-assay accuracy and precision determination to a nearly full validation. Typical bioanalytical method modifications or changes that fall into this category include, but are not limited to, the following: Bioanalytical method transfers between laboratories; Changes in analytical methodology (e.g., a change in detection systems); Changes in sample processing procedures; Changes in sample volume...

rules.rule_text: Cross validation is required when sample analyses within a single study are conducted at more than one site or more than one laboratory. Cross validation must be conducted with shared matrix QCs and nonpooled subject samples at each site or laboratory to establish interlaboratory reliability.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.9.b, Cross Validation
rules.verbatim_quote: Cross validation is necessary when sample analyses within a single study are conducted at more than one site or more than one laboratory. In such cases, cross validation with shared matrix QCs and nonpooled subject samples should be conducted at each site or laboratory to establish interlaboratory reliability.
rules.exceptions: Pooled incurred samples can be used when insufficient volume exists.

rules.rule_text: The sponsor must appropriately characterize and document (determine identity, purity, and stability) all reference standards and critical reagents, including antibodies, labeled analytes, and matrices, and store them under defined conditions. When there are changes to critical reagents (lot-to-lot changes or switches), the sponsor must evaluate binding, re-optimize assays, verify performance with a standard curve and QCs, and evaluate cross-reactivities.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.1.b, Critical Reagents
rules.verbatim_quote: The sponsor should appropriately characterize and document (i.e., determine the identity, purity and stability) the critical reagents, including – but not limited to – any reference standards, antibodies, labeled analytes, and matrices... Assay validation is important when there are changes to the critical reagents, such as lot-to-lot changes or switches to another reagent. For example, if there are changes to the labeled analytes, detector reagents, or antibodies, the sponsor should: Evaluate binding and re-optimize assays; Verify performance with a standard curve and QCs; Evaluate cross-reactivities.

rules.rule_text: The calibration standards must be prepared in the same biological matrix as the samples in the intended study. A calibration curve must be generated for each analyte in the sample.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.2, Calibration Curve
rules.verbatim_quote: The sponsor should prepare the calibration standards in the same biological matrix as the samples in the intended study. Study samples may contain more than one analyte. The sponsor should generate a calibration curve for each analyte in the sample.
rules.exceptions: When surrogate matrices are necessary, the sponsor must justify and validate the calibration curves.

rules.rule_text: QCs must be prepared in the same matrix as the study samples. Calibration standards and QCs must be prepared from separate stock solutions. Calibrators and QCs must be made up in lots of blank matrix that is free of interference or matrix effects.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.3, Quality Control Samples
rules.verbatim_quote: Sponsors should prepare QCs in the same matrix as the study samples to be assayed with the validated method... The sponsor should prepare any calibration standards and QCs from separate stock solutions... The sponsor should make up calibrators and QCs in lots of blank matrix that is free of interference or matrix effects.
rules.exceptions: If the sponsor can demonstrate precision and accuracy in one validation run using calibrators and QCs prepared from separate stock solutions, then calibrators and QCs prepared from the same stock solution may be used in subsequent runs.

rules.rule_text: For LC/MS methods, the sponsor must determine the effects of the matrix on ion suppression, ion enhancement, or extraction efficiency. Internal standards must be assessed to avoid interference with the analyte.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.4, Selectivity and Specificity
rules.verbatim_quote: When using liquid chromatography/mass spectrometry (LC/MS) methods, the sponsor or applicant should determine the effects of the matrix on ion suppression, ion enhancement, or extraction efficiency. Internal standards should be assessed to avoid interference with the analyte.

rules.rule_text: During validation, the sponsor must confirm that the assay is free of potential interfering substances including endogenous matrix components, metabolites, and anticipated concomitant medications. If the study sample contains more than one analyte intended to be quantified by different methods, each method must be tested for interference from the other analyte.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.4, Selectivity and Specificity
rules.verbatim_quote: During validation, the sponsor should confirm that the assay is free of potential interfering substances including endogenous matrix components, metabolites, anticipated concomitant medications, etc. If the study sample contains more than one analyte and the analytes are intended to be quantified by different methods, the sponsor should test each method for interference from the other analyte.

rules.rule_text: When biomarker data will be used to support a regulatory decision making, such as the pivotal determination of safety and/or effectiveness or to support dosing instructions in product labeling, the assay must be fully validated.
rules.source_section: Section V.B, Biomarkers
rules.verbatim_quote: When biomarker data will be used to support a regulatory decision making, such as the pivotal determination of safety and/or effectiveness or to support dosing instructions in product labeling, the assay should be fully validated.
rules.exceptions: For assays intended to support early drug development (candidate selection, go-no-go decisions, proof-of-concept), the sponsor may determine the appropriate extent of validation.

rules.rule_text: Documentation at the analytical site must include general and specific SOPs. All relevant documentation necessary for reconstructing the study must be maintained in a secure environment. Records must be contemporaneous with the event; subsequent alterations must not obscure the original data. The basis for changing or reprocessing data must be documented with sufficient detail, and the original record must be maintained.
rules.source_section: Section VI, Documentation
rules.verbatim_quote: General and specific SOPs and good record keeping are essential to a properly validated analytical method... Regardless of the documentation format (i.e., paper or electronic), records should be contemporaneous with the event, and subsequent alterations should not obscure the original data. The basis for changing or reprocessing data should be documented with sufficient detail, and the original record should be maintained.

rules.rule_text: The use of two different bioanalytical technologies for the development of a drug (e.g., for BE studies in ANDAs) is discouraged. When a new platform is used, data must be bridged using at least 20 incurred samples.
rules.source_section: Section V.D, Bridging Data From Multiple Bioanalytical Technologies
rules.verbatim_quote: The use of two different bioanalytical technologies for the development of a drug may generate data for the same product that could be difficult to interpret... Therefore, when a new platform is used in the development of a drug, the data it produces should be bridged to that of the other method. This is best accomplished by assessing the output of both methods with a set of incurred samples (a minimum of 20 samples)... The use of two methods for BE studies in ANDAs is discouraged.
rules.exceptions: Sponsors are encouraged to seek feedback from the appropriate FDA review division early in drug development.
